scenario.context: Station 01: component project with follow-up assignments on design, algorithms, quality, and delivery.
scenario.mission: You handle the kickoff task and several follow-up tasks along a component project with increasing responsibility.

station.profile: Modularization, data structures, algorithms, testing, and documentation
constraints: Keep the solution maintainable and traceable
Treat tests and documentation as mandatory parts

customerRequest.subject: Station 01: Prioritization module with follow-up tasks
customerRequest.body:
Please start developing a prioritization module for a service portal.

After the kickoff, further tasks on algorithms, data integration, testing, and delivery will follow.

questions.title: Start modularization
questions.prompt: Which design decision makes the most sense for the start?

options.text: Put everything into one class
Separate responsibilities by component
Create only filenames, no structure

feedback.correct: Correct: Separating responsibilities is central.
feedback.wrong: Not suitable: Without clear module boundaries the solution becomes unstable.

questions.title: UML and clean code
questions.prompt: Which points support a sound design? (Multiple choice)

options.text: Make dependencies visible in the UML diagram
Use clear method names and short functions
Leave side effects undocumented
Use patterns only when they provide real benefit
Define interfaces only after implementation
options.rationale: Too late for a clean design.

feedback.correct: Correct: Design and code quality are linked cleanly.
feedback.wrong: Partly wrong: Please give more weight to UML transparency and clean code.

questions.title: Data structure for priorities
questions.prompt: Which structure fits best for prioritized retrieval?

options.text: Unsorted list
Priority structure with a defined order
Arbitrary set without order

feedback.correct: Correct: Prioritized retrieval needs an appropriate structure.
feedback.wrong: Not suitable: The structure has to support the access pattern.

questions.title: Python and Java structures
questions.prompt: Which statements make professional sense? (Multiple choice)

options.text: Maps are suitable for fast key access
Sets make sense for unique values
Lists are always unsuitable
options.rationale: Lists are useful in many cases.

options.text: Language-specific libraries have to be considered
options.rationale: Python and Java differ in their ecosystems.

options.text: Justifying the structure choice is optional

feedback.correct: Correct: Selection and justification are practical.
feedback.wrong: Partly wrong: Please connect benefits, limits, and language context more clearly.

questions.title: From design to implementation
questions.prompt: Put the flow for a viable implementation in order.

feedback.correct: Correct: The flow is methodically sound.
feedback.wrong: Not ideal: Please clarify and design first, then implement and evaluate.

questions.title: Implementation effort
questions.prompt: A module has 7 subtasks of 28 minutes each. How many minutes is the total effort?

feedback.correct: Calculated correctly.
feedback.wrong: Please multiply quantity and duration correctly.

questions.title: Justify the design decision
questions.prompt: Justify the module and data-structure decision in 4-6 sentences.
questions.expectedOutline: Sound justification of structural decisions with links to maintainability and runtime.

feedback.correct: Correct: The decision is justified in a traceable way.
feedback.wrong: Please make the module and data-structure references more concrete.

questions.title: Secure algorithm quality
questions.prompt: Which points help make algorithms robust? (Multiple choice)

options.text: Test edge cases explicitly
Define clear termination conditions for recursion
Choose a sorting method without data context
Estimate runtime impact for typical data volumes
Ignore failure cases

feedback.correct: Correct: This strengthens stability and performance.
feedback.wrong: Partly wrong: Please pay more attention to edge cases, termination conditions, and runtime impact.

questions.title: Choose a sorting method
questions.prompt: Which decision makes the most sense for large, unsorted ticket volumes?

options.text: Choose a method without considering runtime
Choose the sorting method based on data volume and stability requirements
Always use the same method

feedback.correct: Correct: Methods must fit the data profile.
feedback.wrong: Not suitable: Choosing a method needs context.

questions.title: Follow-up task 1 - Deepen algorithms and recursion
Structure the recursion flow
questions.prompt: Put the steps for recursive processing in order.

feedback.correct: Correct: The recursion is structured cleanly.
feedback.wrong: Not ideal: Termination condition and subproblem have to be clear first.

questions.title: Estimate sorting time
questions.prompt: A sorting step needs 4 ms per round. With 38 rounds, how many ms in total?

feedback.correct: Calculated correctly.
feedback.wrong: Please multiply the number of rounds and the duration correctly.

questions.title: Algorithm refactoring
questions.prompt: Which measures improve the algorithm? (Multiple choice)

options.text: Break complex steps into clearly named helper functions
Avoid duplicate calculations
Keep recursion without a base case
Choose the data structure to match the access pattern
Test only the happy path

feedback.correct: Correct: The refactoring is professionally sound.
feedback.wrong: Partly wrong: Please consider stability and efficiency together.

questions.title: Recursion vs iteration
questions.prompt: When is a recursive solution especially useful?

options.text: When subproblems are clearly self-similar
options.rationale: That is a typical recursion case.

options.text: When there is no termination condition
When readability does not matter

feedback.correct: Correct: A self-similar problem structure is a good signal.
feedback.wrong: Not suitable: Recursion needs structure and termination logic.

questions.title: Document algorithm decision
questions.prompt: Write in 4-6 sentences why the chosen method fits the load situation.
questions.expectedOutline: Context reference through data volume, runtime impact, and justified choice.

feedback.correct: Correct: The decision is technically traceable.
feedback.wrong: Please connect data volume, runtime, and decision more clearly.

questions.title: Follow-up task 2 - Compression, cryptography, and hashing
Classify hashing correctly
questions.prompt: Which statement about hash functions is correct?

options.text: Hashing is used for integrity checks and is not reversible
Hashing replaces every encryption
options.rationale: Wrong: integrity and confidentiality are different.

options.text: Hashing compresses data automatically

feedback.correct: Correct: The integrity aspect is central.
feedback.wrong: Not suitable: Separate the purposes of hashing, encryption, and compression.

questions.title: Security-oriented implementation
questions.prompt: Which points make sense in implementation? (Multiple choice)

options.text: Choose suitable encryption for transport or storage
Store a hash value for integrity checks
Treat compression as the only security measure
Handle failure cases for key or token problems
Build crypto logic without documentation

feedback.correct: Correct: Technology and security goal fit together.
feedback.wrong: Partly wrong: Please secure protection needs and failure cases more strongly.

questions.title: Compression factor
questions.prompt: A file shrinks from 1200 KB to 780 KB. How large is the saving in KB?

feedback.correct: Calculated correctly.
feedback.wrong: Please subtract the original and target sizes correctly.

questions.title: Security chain in the module
questions.prompt: Put the flow for secure data transfer in order.
items: Generate a hash for integrity checks
correctOrder: Generate a hash for integrity checks

feedback.correct: Correct: The flow is consistent and security-oriented.
feedback.wrong: Not ideal: Please stage validation, protection, and verification cleanly.

questions.title: Follow-up task 3 - JSON, web service, and database
Integration stability
questions.prompt: Which measures make the integration more robust? (Multiple choice)

options.text: Validate the JSON structure before processing
Plan a timeout and retry strategy for the web service
Ignore API errors if a response arrives
Align DB write operations with the API status
Take over version changes without testing

feedback.correct: Correct: The integration logic is built robustly.
feedback.wrong: Partly wrong: Please secure validation, error handling, and consistency more strongly.

questions.title: Error strategy for web service
questions.prompt: Which response makes sense for HTTP errors?

options.text: Log the error, handle it in a controlled way, and check a business fallback
Ignore the error silently
Terminate the application immediately

feedback.correct: Correct: This is a robust error strategy.
feedback.wrong: Not suitable: Errors need controlled handling.

questions.title: Integration note
questions.prompt: Formulate in 4-6 sentences how JSON, web service, and DB work together reliably.
questions.expectedOutline: Data flow with validation, error strategy, and consistency reference.

feedback.correct: Correct: The integration chain is described in a traceable way.
feedback.wrong: Please connect JSON, the web service, and the DB more concretely.

questions.title: Follow-up task 4 - Testing and documentation delivery
From test plan to unit test
questions.prompt: Put the flow for a clean unit-test process in order.
items: Define a test goal for each component
correctOrder: Define a test goal for each component

feedback.correct: Correct: The test process is structured.
feedback.wrong: Not ideal: Strategy and test-case derivation have to come before automation.

questions.title: Unit-test rate
questions.prompt: Out of 34 unit tests, 29 run successfully. What is the success rate in percent (rounded)?

feedback.correct: Calculated correctly.
feedback.wrong: Please calculate the success share correctly as a percentage.

questions.title: Formulate interface delivery note
questions.prompt: Formulate a delivery note (4-6 sentences) with test status, interface scope, and an open remaining point.
questions.expectedOutline: Clear test status, documented interface scope, and a controlled remaining point.

feedback.correct: Correct: The delivery note is professionally usable.
feedback.wrong: Please structure test status, interface content, and the remaining point more clearly.
